context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about NGOs and other civil society actors in development, and I’m kind of stuck on how to organize it. I think I get the basic idea, but I’m not sure how to answer the “how good are NGOs” part without sounding too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to start with. Before thinking about organization, let's make sure you have something solid to organize. So — in your own words, what do you actually think makes an NGO 'good' or 'not good' as a development agent? What criteria would you use to judge that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of their work and asking for organizational/structural help. I should not provide structure or organization for them, as that would be doing part of the writing work. Instead, I should redirect them toward the substance of the question by asking them to first articulate their own thinking on NGO effectiveness, which is the core of the prompt. This uses guided discovery and keeps the student doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: I guess I’d judge them by whether they actually improve people’s lives, but that feels too vague. Maybe also whether they’re accountable to local communities, whether the results last, and whether they work with or around the state in a useful way?
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid criteria. Now here's a harder question: do you think those criteria always point in the same direction, or can they sometimes conflict with each other? Think about a real or hypothetical NGO situation — can you imagine a case where an NGO scores well on one of your criteria but poorly on another?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered genuinely good criteria - impact, accountability, sustainability, and state relations. These are substantive and relevant to the prompt. I should affirm their direction without flattering them, and push them to deepen one of these criteria rather than staying at the surface level. The Socratic move here is to ask them to think about tensions or trade-offs between these criteria, which will naturally lead toward a more nuanced answer to 'how good are NGOs'.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think they can definitely conflict. Like an NGO might deliver services really fast after a crisis, so it looks effective in the short term, but maybe it isn’t accountable to local people or it weakens local government if everyone depends on the NGO instead.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely important tension you've identified. Now, does that example suggest anything to you about *when* or *under what conditions* NGOs might be more or less effective as development agents?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a real and important tension - efficiency vs. accountability and state-building. This is a substantive insight that connects to key development planning debates. I should validate this and push them to connect it to the prompt's next question: 'in what circumstances are NGOs more effective?' Their example already hints at context-dependency, which is exactly what the prompt is asking about.

exchanges.student: I think maybe NGOs are more effective when there’s an urgent gap the state can’t fill, like after conflict or during disasters. But in long-term development, I’m guessing they’re less effective if they substitute for public institutions instead of helping build local capacity.
exchanges.tutor: You're developing a nuanced argument there. Now the prompt also asks about social movements as civil society actors — how do you see them as different from NGOs in terms of how they engage with development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a coherent argument about context-dependency - crisis vs. long-term development, substitution vs. capacity-building. This is good progress. I should affirm the direction and push them to think about the prompt's next questions: limitations of NGOs more broadly, and the role of social movements. The student hasn't touched social movements at all yet, and that's a significant part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think social movements are different because they usually push for political change from below, not just service delivery or projects. NGOs can be more formal and professionalized, while social movements might be better at representing demands, challenging power, and changing what “development” even means — but I’m not totally sure if that’s too broad.
exchanges.tutor: That distinction is actually quite sharp, not too broad. Here's a follow-up: the prompt asks about the *boundary* of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts. What do you think shapes where that boundary sits — why might civil society have more or less room to operate depending on the context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a meaningful distinction between NGOs and social movements - formalization/professionalization vs. political agency and norm-challenging. This is a strong insight and directly relevant to the prompt. It's not too broad - it's actually quite sharp. I should push them slightly further to think about the boundary question in the prompt: what IS the boundary of civil society as an actor, and how does context shape it? This will help them address the last part of the prompt they haven't touched yet.

exchanges.student: I think the boundary depends a lot on the state and political context — like whether the government allows independent organizing or sees it as a threat. Also maybe donor influence matters, and even local social hierarchies, because not all “civil society” actors represent everyone equally.
exchanges.tutor: You've now worked through all the key parts of the prompt. I think it's time to stop discussing and start writing. Take what you've developed in this conversation and try drafting your reflection. What's your opening argument going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now touched on all major parts of the prompt: NGO effectiveness, context-dependency, limitations, social movements, and civil society boundaries. Their thinking is coherent and substantive. We've now had about 5 exchanges of good conceptual development. Per the rules about sensing redundancy and asking the student to integrate, this is a good moment to ask them to start writing rather than keep discussing. I should not summarize or organize for them - that's their job.

exchanges.student: I think my opening argument is that NGOs matter in development, but they’re not automatically “good” just because they have a development mission. Their effectiveness depends on context, especially whether they are accountable to local communities, whether they strengthen or bypass state capacity, and whether broader civil society actors like social movements can push for deeper structural change.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong opening argument — it sets up a clear position and signals the structure of your reflection. Now stop talking to me and go write your draft. Come back when you have something on paper.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear and well-structured opening argument that synthesizes what they developed in conversation. It's genuinely good. I should affirm it briefly and push them to start actually writing the full draft rather than continuing to discuss. My role now is to keep them moving toward producing text, not to keep refining ideas verbally.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try drafting it now. I might come back once I have a rough version because I’m still not fully confident about balancing NGOs versus social movements.
exchanges.tutor: Good — go write. When you come back with a draft, we can work through whatever feels unbalanced then.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is about to go write, which is exactly what should happen. I should encourage them briefly and not add more content or scaffolding that might substitute for their own thinking. A short send-off is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: Sounds good, thanks. I’ll bring back a draft once I’ve got something down.
exchanges.tutor: Good luck — see you with a draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is signing off to go write. A brief acknowledgment is appropriate. No need to add anything substantive.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I’ll be back with one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is simply confirming they'll return. A very brief acknowledgment is all that's needed. No new content should be introduced.
